Independent Research · Healthcare

Indian Healthcare: A Deep Dive

A look at the Indian healthcare industry today, the seven parts that make it up, and how one fund is set up to invest in them.

By Manzil Sharma · Shri Ram College of Commerce · Research Intern, Healthcare · July 2026

01 · The Landscape

Indian healthcare, right now

India spends far less on health than almost any large economy — and that gap is the whole story. As incomes rise, health spending rises with them. India is near the start of that climb.

3.3%
Health spend as a share of GDP (US: 16.5%, China: 5.4%)
$80
Health spend per person, per year
1.5
Hospital beds per 1,000 people (global average: 2.8)
$163bn
Size of the Indian healthcare market today

Why the gap keeps closing

Healthcare is not a luxury people cut back on. As a country gets richer and older, it spends more on staying well. The United States went from spending about 6% of its economy on health in 1970 to about 17% today. India, at 3.3%, has a long runway ahead.

The kind of illness is changing too. India is shifting from infectious disease toward lifestyle conditions — heart disease, diabetes, cancer. Deaths from heart disease alone rose from 21% to 27% of the total between 2010 and 2019. These conditions need ongoing, higher-value care, which is good for the businesses that provide it.

What is pushing it forward

Five forces are behind the growth: an ageing population, more lifestyle disease, wider health insurance, decades of under-built hospital infrastructure, and India's low-cost edge in making medicines and running research for the world.

A push from policy

The 2026–27 Union Budget backs the same areas: ₹10,000 crore for a biotech manufacturing mission, a large jump in public-health and hospital funding, and support for a national network of clinical trial sites. Government money is flowing into exactly the parts of healthcare that are growing fastest.

India, the pharmacy of the world

India makes about 60% of the world's vaccines and supplies one in five generic medicines by volume. Indian companies now account for roughly 46% of all US drug approvals and have the most US-FDA-cleared factories outside America — at a manufacturing cost about a third lower than Western peers.

02 · The Seven Segments

One word, seven very different businesses

“Healthcare” is not one industry. It is seven, and they behave completely differently — different customers, different capital needs, different growth rates. Click any segment to open it up.

03 · Sectoral Shifts

The healthcare mix is changing

A decade or two ago, investing in Indian healthcare mostly meant investing in pharma. That is no longer true. The centre of gravity has been moving toward hospitals, diagnostics, devices and research services. Here is what that shift looks like.

SHARE OF INDIA'S LISTED HEALTHCARE MARKET VALUE, BY SEGMENT

From one segment to many

In 2000, pharma made up about 89% of India's listed healthcare market. By 2024 that had fallen to roughly 64%. Hospitals and diagnostics, together barely 3% at the start, are now around 17%. Research and manufacturing services (API and CDMO) grew from about 7% to 17%.

The point is not that pharma shrank — it grew a lot. It is that everything else grew faster, so the opportunity today is far broader than it used to be.

Leadership rotates every year

No single segment stays on top. Diagnostics returned a median 211% in one year and −39% in another; medical equipment swung from +167% to −20%. Because you cannot reliably guess which part will lead next, the sensible response is to own good businesses across all seven — not to bet on one.

89% → 64%
Pharma's share of listed healthcare (2000 → 2024)
3% → 17%
Hospitals + diagnostics share
14.0%
Fastest-growing segment today: research & manufacturing services
11.2%
Second fastest: diagnostics
What I take from this

The shift is real and it is one-directional: services, diagnostics and devices keep gaining ground on plain pharma. A healthcare portfolio built for the year 2010 would look far too pharma-heavy for the market of 2026. The interesting question is no longer “which pharma stock?” but “how do you own the whole, broadening sector well?”

04 · The Vehicle

What is an Alternative Investment Fund?

An AIF is a privately pooled fund for large, experienced investors, regulated by SEBI. It is not sold to the general public like a mutual fund, and it comes with more freedom in what it can own.

Three categories, one that fits here

SEBI splits AIFs into three types. Category III — the type used here — can run more flexible strategies and, importantly, is not tied down by the strict diversification and daily-redemption rules that govern mutual funds.

Two freedoms matter for healthcare in particular:

It can hold private companies

Unlike a mutual fund, an AIF can buy shares in companies that are not yet listed on the stock exchange — including businesses about to go public.

It is never forced to sell

A close-ended AIF has no daily redemptions, so the manager can hold through a downturn instead of selling at the worst possible time.

Why this suits healthcare so well

A lot of India's best healthcare businesses are still private. Only a small slice of the hospital, diagnostics and medical-device markets is available on the stock exchange — the rest is owned by companies that have not listed yet. A fund that can buy private shares can reach parts of healthcare that a normal fund simply cannot.

Patience pays here

Healthcare also rewards holding on. Over one year it moves with the market, but over ten years it has been both steadier and stronger. A fund with a multi-year lock-in is built to capture that — it gives the investment time to work.

Put simply: the structure is not a technicality. It is what lets a healthcare fund own the whole opportunity, and hold it long enough to matter.

How it differs from the funds most people know

The same rupee can be invested through very different wrappers. The table below shows why a Category III AIF is the right container for this particular strategy.

FeatureMutual FundPMSCategory III AIF
Who can investEveryone (retail)₹50 lakh minimum₹1 crore minimum (sophisticated investors)
Can hold private companies?NoRarelyYes — incl. pre-IPO
Forced to sell in a panic?Yes (daily redemptions)Client can exit anytimeNo — close-ended with lock-in
Diversification rulesStrict capsFlexibleFlexible
Can use leverage / derivativesLimitedLimitedYes, within limits
The trade-off, stated fairly

These freedoms come with strings: a high entry ticket, a multi-year lock-in and less liquidity than a mutual fund. That is exactly why an AIF suits a patient, high-conviction strategy — and why it is offered only to investors who can accept those terms. It is a tool for a specific job, not a better version of a mutual fund.

Why “Category III” specifically

Category I backs start-ups and infrastructure; Category II covers private equity and debt. Category III is the one built for actively managed, listed-and-unlisted equity strategies — the natural home for a fund that wants to pick great healthcare businesses wherever they trade.

05 · The Fund & The Firm

The WhiteOak Healthcare Opportunities Fund

This is the fund I studied: a close-ended Category III AIF that invests mainly in listed healthcare companies, with room to hold some private ones too. It is measured against the BSE Healthcare index.

Key facts

StructureClose-ended Category III AIF
FocusMainly listed pharma & healthcare, some unlisted
BenchmarkBSE Healthcare Total Return
Term5 years (extendable), 3-year lock-in
ManagerWhiteOak Capital Asset Management

About the firm

WhiteOak was founded in 2017 by Prashant Khemka, who previously ran India and global emerging-market equities at Goldman Sachs Asset Management. The firm's whole identity is stock-picking.

Its belief, in one line: own great businesses at fair prices, and let returns come from picking the right companies — not from betting on which sector is hot. To compare companies fairly across very different segments, it uses its own way of looking at a business, which the next section explains in plain terms.

How the fund is positioned

Put together, the theme, the vehicle and the firm give this fund a distinct place in an investor's portfolio. Four things define it:

01

A pure play on a multi-year theme

Not a short-term trade, but focused exposure to one of India's clearest structural growth stories — healthcare — across all seven of its segments.

02

A genuine diversifier

Healthcare's returns have historically had low correlation with the broad market, so this can steady an overall portfolio rather than simply add more of the same.

03

Built for active stock-picking

Because the seven segments are so different, the gap between the best and worst companies is wide — fertile ground for a research-led manager to add value, rather than just track an index.

04

Access to the whole opportunity

Listed and pre-IPO, backed by an experienced healthcare team — so the fund can own promising private businesses early, not just whatever happens to be on the exchange.

In short

It is positioned as a focused, all-of-healthcare, actively managed vehicle — using the freedom of an AIF to reach the fast-growing, still-private corners of the sector that an ordinary fund cannot.

06 · Methodology

How I assessed the portfolio

I started from a universe of 108 companies screened across the seven segments, filtered it down to the 65 with enough public information to judge properly, and then scored those to shape a sensible ~50-stock line-up alongside the current holdings. Here is the approach, step by step.

108
companies sourced
across all 7 segments
→
65
filtered on public data
(enough to assess)
→
50
scored & sized
into a line-up
1

Filter and sort

From the 108-company universe, I kept the 65 with enough public disclosure to assess fairly. Each was tagged by segment and by listed-or-private.

2

Score the business (the “OpCo”)

How good is the underlying business? I scored each 1–5 on 3-year revenue growth, operating (EBITDA) margins and return on capital (ROCE/ROIC), plus market share, pricing power and export strength.

3

Score the finances (the “FinCo”)

How sound are the finances and management? I scored each 1–5 on return on capital, debt-to-equity, free-cash-flow generation and working-capital intensity, alongside the governance track record.

4

Apply the same bar to private firms

Private companies got the same two scores, using sensible stand-ins: revenue scale, unit economics (hospital ARPOB, lab asset-turns), marquee backers and IPO-readiness (a filed DRHP), and audited numbers where public. A weak FinCo score ruled a name out, however good the business.

5

Label Core or Watch

Strong on both scores → Core (high conviction). Good but needing a closer eye → Watch. Weak on either → dropped. This produced 35 Core and 15 Watch names.

6

Size each segment

Finally I set each segment's weight by balancing three things: where it stands today, how fast it is growing, and keeping the whole portfolio balanced rather than concentrated in a few names.

The metrics at a glance

Business (OpCo): revenue growth · EBITDA margin · ROCE/ROIC · market share · pricing power.
Finance (FinCo): ROCE · debt-to-equity · free cash flow · working capital · governance. Each on a simple 1–5 scale, applied the same way to listed and private names.

An honest caveat

These scores are a first pass by an intern, meant to structure the thinking — not a final investment decision. A real call would need live financials, full company filings and a proper valuation on top of what I've done here.

07 · What I Found

The portfolio, and what I'd adjust

Reading the current fund against the sector shift, one thing stood out: it leans more toward the older, slower parts of healthcare than the market now does. Below is the current mix, a suggested rebalance, and the full scored line-up you can explore.

SEGMENT WEIGHT — CURRENT vs SUGGESTED (% of fund)

The pattern in plain terms

The fund currently holds about 79% in its three biggest, slower-growing segments (pharma, hospitals, API) and very little in the faster ones. My suggestion trims the big three a little and builds up diagnostics, devices and research services — the parts that are gaining share.

Crucially, this is not about chasing hot sectors. It is about not leaving a growing part of healthcare almost entirely un-owned. Medical devices, for example, sat at under 1% of the fund.

50
Companies, split 35 Core / 15 Watch
9
Private companies, a small, capped slice

Filter by segment, listing or quality; click any column to sort. The business and finance scores run 1 (weak) to 5 (excellent).

08 · My Take

What I'd suggest, and why

These are my own conclusions from the research — a starting point, not a final word.

In one sentence

The fund's edge isn't predicting healthcare's future — it's being able to own the growing parts of the sector, including the private ones, and hold them long enough for that to pay off.
